text: Thirty-one years old male patient with one year history of symptomatic fistula in ano periodically swollen and draining pus.
He was referred to our institute two months ago with a sedum in place and a superficial abscess.
Under anesthesia, the surgeon drains the abscess and cleans the fistula, using surgical instruments to remove pus and debris from the affected area.
The diagnosis was confirmed as a trans-sphincteric fistula incorporating about forty percent of the sphincter by MRI.
Then a lift procedure was planned.
The patient is positioned in lithotomy, and the surgeon thoroughly curettes the fistula tract, removing visible granulation tissue.
The surgeon uses a Chelsea Eden anal speculum to visualize the internal opening of the fistula, providing a clear view of the surgical site.
The internal opening of the fistula is identified with a sedum in place, as hydrogen peroxide injection or gentle probing is typically used for this purpose.
The surgeon ties a ureteral catheter to the sedum and inserts it into the fistula's tract, aiding in the identification and dissection of the tract.
The surgeon palpates the intersphincteric plane and delineates it using a cautery device, marking the area for further dissection.
The surgeon makes a curvilinear incision to access the intersphincteric plane, following the delineation of the area with cautery.
The surgeon applies four Lone Star elastic hooks to the anal region, enhancing visualization for the ongoing dissection by retracting surrounding tissue.
The surgeon meticulously dissects the intersphincteric tract using monopolar cautery and a peanut sponge, carefully identifying the tract through sharp and blunt dissection techniques.
The surgeon performs bilateral careful dissection near the external sphincter using mixture forceps, maneuvering around the identified tract.
The surgeon uses a right-angled clamp to hook the intersphincteric tract, carefully directing the tip away from the internal sphincter to prevent perforation.
The surgeon performs further dissection using a peanut sponge and cautery to skeletonize the intersphincteric tract, preparing it for ligation.
The surgeon performs transfixed ligation of the tract at the openings of the external and internal sphincters, securing the sutures with a small clamp.
The surgeon uses a number eleven blade to divide the intersphincteric tract between the sutures after removing the ureteral catheter.
The surgeon places additional sutures to securely ligate the stumps of the inter-sphincteric tract at the internal and external sphincter openings.
The surgeon ensures adequate hemostasis before loosely closing the incision with interrupted polyglactin 3-0 sutures.
The surgeon cores out the distal part of the tract at the external opening to ensure adequate drainage, followed by the injection of local anesthetic into the area to minimize postoperative pain.
